name: System Design — Estimation & Sizing
description: The numbers and rules of thumb every system-design interview leans on. Throughput, storage units, time tricks, R:W ratios, capacity planning, ID space. Pairs with the Latency Numbers deck.

cards.definition: 86,400 — round to **100,000** for back-of-envelope work. Daily volume ÷ 100k ≈ average per-second rate. The single most useful unit-conversion shortcut in an interview.
1 month ≈ 2.5M seconds. 1 year ≈ 31.5M (round to 30M). Useful for converting retention windows to byte budgets.
1 byte. UTF-8 char is 1-4 bytes (BMP basic Latin = 1; emoji = 4). Useful for tweet/SMS/JSON-payload sizing.
16 bytes raw (128 bits); 36 chars as hyphenated string; 22 chars as base64. Matters when an index column is huge × billions of rows.
100-1000 bytes for most app-tier tables. Tweets, comments, events ~300 B; profiles + several columns ~500-1000 B. Multiply by row count for storage estimates.
~200 KB. Useful for sizing image-storage workloads. Original-resolution JPEGs from a phone ~3-5 MB.
~1-2 GB. 1 minute ≈ 100 MB. Drives bandwidth and CDN cost estimates for video products.
~100k ops/sec for typical GET/SET workloads. A single 64 GB Redis instance handles most caching workloads — go distributed when working set exceeds a few hundred GB.
~10-50k qps. Above that, add read replicas. When a single primary can't keep up with writes (or working set won't fit in RAM), shard.
~100 MB/sec. Scale by adding more partitions (one consumer per partition within a group). The partition is the unit of parallelism.
~10-50k rps for a typical Go/Java service with p99 <100 ms. A handy single-instance ceiling — drives 'how many app servers do I need?' answers.
~125 MB/sec (1 Gbps ÷ 8 bits/byte). Useful for checking whether bandwidth is your bottleneck before sharding for throughput. 10 GbE is ~1.25 GB/sec.

cards.term: Memory vs SSD vs disk hierarchy
cards.definition: RAM is **1,000×** faster than SSD; SSD is **100×** faster than spinning disk. Local network is **1,000×** faster than cross-region. Always state where the data lives — it dwarfs almost every other latency factor.

1) Establish user scale (DAU). 2) Estimate request rate (QPS). 3) Separate reads from writes. 4) Estimate storage. 5) Estimate bandwidth. Each step's output drives a design decision; skipping one means an arbitrary choice.
20-40%. ~20% for social products (people don't visit every day); ~40% for utilities (banking, calendar). Lets you derive DAU from a published MAU number.
5-10× (use 10× as a safe default). A flat hourly average understates the peak hour by an order of magnitude — design for peak, not average, or you'll thrash at noon.
Avg RPS = (DAU × requests_per_user_per_day) ÷ 86,400. Peak RPS = Avg × peak_factor. Then split into read RPS and write RPS using the read:write ratio.
Storage = writes/day × bytes/record × retention_days × replication_factor. Quote both raw and replicated numbers (3× is a common replication factor).
1 KB/s = 86.4 GB/day. 1 MB/s = 86.4 TB/day. 1 GB/s = 86.4 PB/day. Multiply per-second bandwidth by 86.4 for daily; use it the other way to size egress costs.
~100:1. Drives heavy caching investment and read replicas. Write throughput rarely the bottleneck; the fan-out on read is.
~1000:1. Index updates (writes) are rare relative to queries (reads). Justifies expensive index structures (inverted index, large in-memory caches).
~1:1. Roughly every message sent is read once (or close to it). Symmetric design — neither read nor write side dominates.
~1:100 (write-heavy). Events flow in continuously; queries are rare and aggregated. Justifies columnar stores (Parquet, ClickHouse) and async batch ingestion.
~10,000:1. Reads are constant background polls; writes are operator changes. Aggressive in-memory caching with versioned keys is the default.

cards.term: Rule of thumb: service instance capacity
cards.definition: A single well-tuned HTTP service instance handles ~10k rps. Divide your peak read RPS by ~10k to get a baseline instance count, round up generously for headroom + multi-AZ.

cards.term: When to add a read replica
cards.definition: Add a replica every ~5-10× read scale. The first replica gives the biggest win (offloads ~half the read load). Beyond ~5 replicas, replication lag and connection sprawl start to hurt.
When a single node can't hold the working set in RAM, OR can't keep up with writes. Sharding is expensive (cross-shard joins, rebalancing, hot keys) — defer until vertical scaling + replicas exhaust.
Fit the hot dataset in RAM if at all possible — RAM access is 1,000× faster than SSD. If your working set exceeds a few hundred GB on one host, distributed cache or accept misses.
n-char alphanumeric (62 chars: a-z, A-Z, 0-9) = 62^n combinations. n-char lowercase = 26^n. Drives the 'how short can our short URL be?' choice.

6 alphanumeric chars = ~56 billion (bit.ly scale). 8 chars = ~218 trillion. 10 chars = ~141 trillion lowercase-only (26¹⁰), up to ~8.4×10¹⁷ alphanumeric (62¹⁰). Long enough that random collision is statistically zero.

cards.term: URL shortener — what the numbers tell you
cards.definition: 100M DAU, 1% create / day, 5 clicks / day → 10 writes/sec, 5k reads/sec, ~1 TB over 5 years. Conclusions: 500:1 R:W → cache aggressively; trivial writes → single primary fine; 1 TB → no sharding yet.

Interviewers grade your reasoning, not your number. Write the assumptions on the board first ('100M DAU, 10% peak hour, 100B avg payload') so the calculation is auditable and the numbers are defensible.
Use 100k for 86,400. 1B for 987M. 30M for 31.5M. The estimate is a sanity check — precision adds noise without insight, and slows the conversation.

Don't just produce a number — connect it to a design choice. 'Reads 500× writes → add a cache layer' beats 'I'll add Redis'. The number justifies the architecture; the architecture without the number is a guess.

Does the result match the system class you have in mind? 10 PB for a small app means you have an error. 100 GB for global Twitter means you have an error. Cross-check by working from the answer back to the assumptions.
